speaker: interviewer
text: Strawczynski, nee Rychter. We left off talking about liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I told you that we were in a place of-- in an apartment of two Polish ladies that new my mother from before the war. And it was early morning, the 17th of January, and I saw my first Russian tanks. And a soldier jumped off and ran and knocked on the door. It was a-- very close, the-- the closest apartment to the street. He was a short Asian soldier, a [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH] or I don't know. He was short. He had typical oriental face. He came because he was thirsty, and he was in a terrible hurry. He wanted water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they gave him water, and he drank. And he ran back after his tank. But this sight is one of the few that will be etched in my mind always. For me, this was liberation. This was freedom. It was all in this little Russian soldier-- Soviet soldier, not even Russian, who was standing there and drinking water. I was very, very moved by-- by that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, so the war ended, and a few survivors started to come back. We stayed with my mother. I went to school. The Jewish Committee was formed. We got all types of help. And life was sort of picked up, you know, from there on. A lot of people--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it possible to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it possible?

speaker: interviewer
text: --make a new life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, obviously, it was. [LAUGHS] How it was possible, I don't know. But obviously life is stronger than anything else. And you make a new life. We live in a place in a room, also together with a few other young women. My mother was the only one that was older. And, you know, in way it was very, very important for us. Because the-- the Russians liked young girls. And they were trying to talk them into sleeping with them. They were not raping us, no. The rapes, of course, we know about them in Germany and other places. But here, they were hungry for-- for love, for women, for everything. They-- they had a terrible, terrible war. You know how many of them killed-- were killed, were died. Anyhow, my mother was the one who protected us, and they listened to her. They would bring us food. They would bring us drinks sometimes, you know. They would drink, and we would just sit there, listen to their songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what I wanted to ask you is, what was the response of the non-Jewish-- just the whole Polish-- to the survivor, the Jewish survivors of the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the war was not really finished for the Poles yet. The Russians came in. They were not welcomed at all. For them, they were-- yeah, they liberated them from the-- from the worst. But this meant a new occupation. They knew what it meant. They identified the Jews very strongly with Communism. And, of course, there was a lot of-- of deprived people during the war, people who were wild, people who were used to death, to-- to killing, to anything. So there were many, many anti-Semitic excesses after the war during that time at the very beginning. However, I went to school. And at school, I had some friends. I had two Polish girls, very, very nice, who befriended me. I-- anyhow, to make the whole story a little bit shorter, my mother got sick. She was sick already, first, you know, after the camp and after that war. And she got sicker and sicker. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Heartbroken.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it turned out that she had cancer that was already spread and neglected, of course. And uterine cancer and she died in March-- no, in April of 1946. April-- April 18, 1946. So I was left alone. I must I've been pretty callous myself at that point. And, you know, I told you, people were so used to what was going on. Everybody lost everybody else. So I was alone too. I-- I remember this little scene when I was interviewed for a-- I applied for a bursary to go to the university. And I was called, and this nice man, probably one of the teachers or clerks in the ministry-- I don't remember-- was talking to me and asking. And then as I was telling him that, well, I was in the ghetto. And I was here, and I was there. And I-- and now my mother died not so long ago, a few months ago. He looked at me with horror. And he-- and real pity. And he said, my god, you know, you're so young, and-- and I looked at him. And I shrugged my shoulders. And I said, well, no, I'm like everybody else around me. He was amazed. He couldn't understand it. And this is how I was, I think. I-- I stayed in Czestochowa until I matriculated. I did two years. I mean, in two years, I did the time at year of high school. There were many schools for adults. A lot of Poles didn't have any possibility to educate themself either. Then after that, I-- I went to university in Lodz Medical School.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the motivating factor to have-- going to medical?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I thought about. Maybe there were none. Maybe I just thought it would be a good idea to be a doctor. But I always wanted to be a-- a-- a pediatrician. And I-- I never forgot it. The Jews get the Warsaw ghetto, and the Jewish beggars. And maybe this influenced me. My brother, also, enrolled in a medical school. I don't know why. However, in-- in Lodz, I lived in so-called so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. This was a home for Jewish young people returning from all over, from camps, from Russia. It was supported by the Jewish committee, by American Jews, basically. And we-- this was a good time. This was a time when we found a family. Everybody was around everybody. And we had a good time together, and we never talked about whatever. We didn't know each other's, you know, camps or ghettos or whatever. This was somewhere there that either we wanted to forget, or we were too busy-- too busy. Most of these people were studying. Many worked. And I spent only one year there, but I met Oscar there, my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give us his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oscar Strawczynski, born in 1918. He's an engineer. We met in this Lodz [NON-ENGLISH]. I stayed there between nine-- in-- from 1947 to 1948. In 1948, Oscar went to-- to Israel, to Palestine or Israel, newly formed Israel, due to fights there in his new country. But he came back after a year, after the war was over. And he fought with the Haganah. And he came back, and then we got married. We moved to Warsaw, so I transferred to the Warsaw Medical School. And this is where we lived. We decided to live in Poland. We supported the-- the new government, the new system. We did believe, like my parents did, that this is what is going to bring freedom. And no exploitation and a wonderful world forever. Well, we were naive, however. We lived there. My daughter was born in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Eva. She works. She-- she was 5 when we came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the motivating factor to come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I think that the motivating factor was growing disenchantment with, and knowledge, what is really behind the system. Disenchantment, and the fact that we had a child, and the memories of the war started to come and to be more and more vivid. My husband survived in Russia, but his memory-- he lost the entire family also. He came back. He didn't find anybody. So we started to think about how safe would it be to be here. It's not a safe country, not only for Jews. It's just not a safe country, period. You know, the terrible history. And my husband had a-- family in Canada, also from Poland. But they left immediately after the war. Two of them survived Treblinka.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in 1959, with our 5-year-old daughter-- and I have been a doctor since 1952, a pediatrician in Poland-- we left. And we came to Montreal. And were there any additions to the family once you arrived in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. For a long time, there were none. I waited 15 years between one child and the other. And it took me time for me to get used to Canada, to-- to get into internship and residency, to repeat my entire-- almost entire training to pass all the exams. It took five years altogether. And then in 1968, my son was born. His name is David. And I've been working, right from the beginning after I finished all my exams. I-- I became a-- a pediatrician and then a pediatric rheumatologist at the Montreal Children's Hospital, a member of the faculty, an associate professor of pediatrics. And this is where we are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, coming to Canada was a shock. It was such a displacement, not only coming from one country or one continent to another, but linguistic and cultural displacement. And it was a shock to meet the Canadian Jews who didn't know much about what was going wrong. They were very different. They looked at us either indifferently or with some suspicion. How come you survived this type of thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: However, well, they just didn't know. They-- you know, maybe it's good that they did know. You need some completely abnormal imagination to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a very good point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So this is my story.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to ask-- first of all, thank you very much for your most comprehensive and detailed testimony and for the emotional input that you've put into the testimony. And one or two concluding questions, if you will-- what do you think the main effects of your experiences have been upon you as a person, as a parent? Could you tell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes, certainly I've been thinking about it more and more as I get older. And I realize more-- more and more as I get older to what extent all this affected me. It was inescapable. Well, constant chronic anxiety. I always expecting a-- a loss. There are so many losses. Always expecting the worst thing to happen. This is-- this is something that my children, of course, felt very strongly and it affected them, I know. Constant anxiety over their safety. Every little illness, every little delay in coming home was something happened. The scenarios that I am still making constantly for my children and my entire family is incredible. This is how it is. And so, in a way, yeah, I was very deeply affected. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your Communist beliefs? Were they affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, this was long ago. This was long ago, yeah. I've learned something since. Yeah. And this is something that I learned and I think I-- I wish all young people are not to-- not to believe in-- not to follow, to question, to question, to question.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would be your message to the next generation?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would be my message, yeah. Well, if I have any message. However, having said all this about how the war affected me, I must say that I never felt like a victim. I never really thought about myself as a victim. And I hate and I resent when people talk to me as if I were a victim. I spoke very little about what I went through. I hate the exploitation of-- of the Holocaust. And I only talk about it when I'm really asked with interest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. To the right of me stands my daughter Eva. She's my first born, born in 1953. Next to her is my husband, Oscar, born in 1918, still working full time-- part time, I'm sorry. Then there is David. David was born in 1968. And he is now looking for a job since he's finishing his master's degree, just finishing schools. And next to him is his dear friend, and our dear friend, Rosemary. Rosemary's name is Rosemary Barrett. She's from Montreal. They've been together for three or almost four years. And she's now finishing her degree in social work. This is an old picture. It shows a group of friends. My father is standing to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: His full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father David and my mother Felicja or Fella, sitting in the middle in the white blouse. This was made in Czestochowa, probably shortly after they met around the year of 1919, maybe, or 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fella [INAUDIBLE] and David Rychter. Czestochowa, 1922 or around. Standing from the back at the left is my father. Next to him is my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fella; my father, David; my mother, Fella; my Uncle Izyk; my other uncle Morris; my third Uncle Hersch; my grandfather sitting, Lajb; the youngest brother in the family, Reuven; and my grandmother, Esther; and in front of my father, there is Manche, the one who survived the war--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --his father. The family name was Rychter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excellent, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my Mother Fella Rychter with her firstborn, Henryk Rychter, made in Czestochowa in 1923 or beginning of 1924. This is Czestochowa, my mother with her two children. On the left is myself at the age of three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name, Hanna, Hanna Rychter. On the other side of my mother is Henryk Rychter, my brother who was then 7, 7 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fella. My mother's name was Fella, Felicja. This is family Rychter in Czestochowa in 1930. Starting from the right is the youngest brother of my father, Rueven or Romik or Reven Rychter. Next to him is my mother, Fella. Standing behind her is my brother Henryk. I don't know the lady sitting at the table. Next to her is my grandmother, Esther. Then sitting is the wife of my Uncle Izyk, and her name was [INAUDIBLE]. Behind her is her child. I don't know the name. And in the middle standing is Manche, one of the uncles, Manche Rychter. This is my father, David Rychter from Czestochowa. The picture was taken in Warsaw in 1934.

speaker: interviewer
text: The family, we gonna go--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the left, there's a picture of my mother Fella Rychter with myself, Hanna Rychter in Czestochowa in 1935. On the right side, it is myself, Hanna Rychter with my brother Henryk Rychter. This was taken in the country during summer vacation and the year was 1936. And this is the picture of a school orchestra, marching through the town of Czestochowa. My brother is playing the trumpet, my brother Henryk. He's third from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the school was the Jewish Gymnasium. This is the picture of my family in Warsaw in 1938 during summer vacation. On the left is my brother Henryk. Next is my mother Fella, my father David, and myself, Hanna Rychter. This picture was found in the mud after the war. It is damaged. This is a picture from-- taken in Warsaw in 1939. It's the last picture of my father. On the left is my brother in a uniform the day-- he had to wear for the class of military training. And on the right, it is myself. And this is the last one from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I said the name, Henryk Rychter, David Rychter, and Hanna Rychter. This is the last picture of my brother, Henryk. This was made in Czestochowa in 1941. He's 17 here. And he just came back walking from Bialystok to Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henryk Rychter.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother Henryk was a partisan. Henryk Rychter, he was killed in March, March 19, 1943. And he was posthumuously decorated with this order in 1948 by the Polish government. This picture was taken in Czestochowa immediately after the war. And it shows the ruins of the ghetto in Czestochowa and the survivors walking through the ruins. OK. This is a picture taken two months after the liberation. It's my mother is on the left, Fella Rychter. I am on the right, Hanna Rychter. And this is following the disinterment of the bodies of my brother. And his part is on [INAUDIBLE]. There are six names there. And the text says, glory to the-- to the young heroes. Something of the sort. This is a picture taken in Warsaw in 1949, after my husband, Oscar Strawczynski, who is on the right, returned from fighting in Haganah in Israel. I am in the middle. I am Hanna Rychter. And to the left is a friend. This is a picture from Poland. We were on vacation in the Polish mountains [INAUDIBLE]. To the right is my daughter, Eva Strawczynski, then my husband, Oscar Strawczynski, and Hanna Strawczynski, myself. These are our children. This is in Canada. To the left there is Eva Strawczynski, age 15 here or 16, and David Strawczynski, one year old. This is a picture of our family in our home in the garden of our home in 1982. To the left is my daughter Eva, then my husband Oscar, my son David, and myself, Hanna Strawczynski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken in Montreal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Here in Montreal in our garden. This is 1982 in Montreal. It's the graduation from college of my daughter Eva. From the left is my son David, my husband Oscar, then Eva with her diploma, and myself, Hannah Strawczynski.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is 1985. It's the matriculation of my is son David Strawczynski who is in the middle. My husband Oscar is on the left. And I am on the right, and it's an important picture for me because I'm wearing a wig here, just recovering from one year of very, very serious illness, which I also survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wall hanging made by my father back in 1938. He did this type of things for money, to earn a little bit more money. And it represents a-- a picture by a famous Polish painter, Stryjenska, called the 'The Polish Autumn,' 'The Golden Polish Autumn.' It's made from felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father who did it was David-- David-- David Rychter, I'm sorry.
